name: Datadog MCP Server (EU)

auth.notes: Two-mode auth at FMFX: (a) local interactive users — OAuth 2.0 per-user flow, personal identity binding; (b) Docker sandbox environments — DD_API_KEY + DD_APPLICATION_KEY HTTP headers, where the underlying Datadog application key is scoped to a read-only custom role and the secret is injected at container start by Keeper Secrets Manager (KSM). Tool access also gated by Datadog resource permissions; the Standard Role grants both mcp_read and mcp_write by default and is NOT used — engineers and the sandbox service principal both ride a custom role with mcp_read only.

retention: Logs/metrics/traces retention follows the FMFX Datadog org's configured retention (typically 15 days hot for logs, 15 months for metrics). MCP request/response payloads are subject to Bits AI / MCP-server processing logs at Datadog's discretion — retention not separately documented.
compensating_controls: Deny-by-default on all mutating tool patterns (create_*, update_*, upsert_*, delete_*, edit_*, append_*, sync_*, execute_*, link_*, add_comment_*, *_onboarding, source_map_uploads, synthetics_test_wizard) via managed settings.json — see settings.json in this folder.

Permission gating at the Datadog tenant: a custom role granting mcp_read but NOT mcp_write applies to BOTH engineer OAuth users AND the sandbox service principal whose app key is injected via KSM. The Standard Role (which grants both) is not used.
Local interactive users authenticate via OAuth 2.0 (personal identity binding) — misuse attributable to a named engineer and revocable in <5 minutes via Datadog admin console.

Docker sandbox environments inject DD_API_KEY + DD_APPLICATION_KEY at container start from Keeper Secrets Manager (KSM); the underlying Datadog application key is scoped to a read-only custom role; key rotation is centrally managed in KSM rather than in container images or env files.
Restrict to EU endpoint (mcp.datadoghq.eu) only — matches FMFX Datadog tenant region.
Re-snapshot before every plugin version bump that touches the allowlist; any new mutating tool surfaces in the diff.

Quarterly review of Datadog audit logs for unexpected MCP activity (mcp.datadoghq.eu requests, especially failed attempts on denied verbs and any traffic from the sandbox service principal outside expected hours).

research_gaps.reason: MCP server request/response logging retention not documented separately from the Datadog Bits AI policy; ask Datadog CSM for written confirmation.
Datadog audit log retention varies by plan tier (Pro vs Enterprise). Confirm FMFX tier-specific retention with platform team.

Sub-processor list sourced verbatim from https://www.datadoghq.com/legal/subprocessors/ on 2026-05-06. Datadog can update the list with notice — platform team should subscribe to the page and re-snapshot on change.

mcp_specific.name_collision_notes: search_* and get_* verbs are heavily overloaded — all 18 Datadog toolsets use them. If any other MCP server is loaded alongside this one (e.g. another vendor's search_logs / get_metric), the model could call the wrong tool. Tool names ARE Datadog-prefixed (search_datadog_*, get_datadog_*) which mitigates collisions, but apm_*, ddsql_*, ndm_* prefixes are not Datadog-namespaced and could collide with future MCPs.

credentials.storage_location: Local users: OAuth 2.0 tokens persisted by Claude Code's MCP OAuth flow in the OS-managed credential store (per-user). Sandbox: DD_API_KEY + DD_APPLICATION_KEY are NOT persisted on disk — injected as environment variables at Docker container start from Keeper Secrets Manager (KSM); the underlying Datadog application key is scoped to a read-only custom role.
credentials.revocation_mechanism: Local OAuth: revoke per-user grant via Datadog Personal Settings → Integrations or org admin via Organization Settings (≤5 min). Sandbox API+App key: rotate via KSM (centrally managed) and revoke the application key in Datadog Organization Settings → Application Keys (≤15 min including KSM propagation).

local_execution.filesystem_access_notes: Remote vendor service over HTTPS — no local subprocess, no filesystem access. Claude Code's MCP HTTP client handles transport in-process.

observability.vendor_audit_log_retention: Datadog Audit Trail — retention depends on FMFX plan tier (typically 15 months on Enterprise). Captures user, IP, action, target for tenant operations.
indirect_injection_vectors: search_datadog_logs / search_datadog_events output — log lines and event messages may contain attacker-controlled strings (URLs, prompts) the model may act on if asked to summarise.
get_datadog_incident / search_datadog_cases output — case titles and comments are user-authored free text.
search_datadog_rum_events output — RUM events include URLs and user-input fields from real browser traffic.
ddsql_run_query results — arbitrary SQL output can include attacker-controlled rows from log/event tables.
ddsql_create_link returns a clickable link — model may surface as actionable URL.

search_datadog_security_signals / get_datadog_security_signal / analyze_datadog_security_signals output — security signal payloads can contain attacker-controlled strings from the original detection source (logs, events, network traffic).

sub_processors: Amazon Web Services, Inc. (US, AU, IT, JP, UK)
compliance.data_residency: Tenant data resides in the EU (Frankfurt) — endpoint is mcp.datadoghq.eu. Sub-processors are partly outside the EU (most are US-based; AWS and Google have multi-region presence; Reversing Labs is CH). Bits AI inference (OpenAI/Anthropic) routes to US. Net: data residency is EU at rest, but cross-border transfer to US sub-processors occurs in normal operation — covered by Datadog's DPA / SCCs (https://www.datadoghq.com/legal/subprocessors/).

stride.spoofing: OAuth bearer-token theft from a developer machine impersonates that engineer until revocation (≤5 min). For sandbox containers, an attacker who pulls DD_API_KEY/DD_APPLICATION_KEY from a running container env or a compromised KSM session impersonates the read-only sandbox service principal until KSM rotation (≤15 min).
stride.tampering: Without write-tool denylist, an attacker (or prompt-injected model) could create monitors/dashboards/feature-flag changes/synthetic tests/notebooks/workflow executions; deny-by-default mitigates this.
stride.repudiation: Datadog Audit Trail records the OAuth subject for every API call — personal identity binding gives strong attribution; shared API+App keys would weaken this and are discouraged.
stride.info_disclosure: MCP can return logs/traces/RUM events that may contain customer PII or secrets accidentally captured by application logging — confidential exposure to the model and via Bits AI to OpenAI/Anthropic sub-processors.
stride.denial: execute_datadog_workflow could be abused to fire production workflows (deploys, runbooks); mass create_datadog_monitor calls could exhaust the org's monitor quota; both blocked by deny list.
stride.elevation: If mcp_write is granted to engineers in the Datadog tenant, a prompt-injected session could escalate to dashboard deletion / monitor mutation / case modification across the entire Datadog org — restrict at the Datadog role level, not just the MCP allowlist.
